text: Wow, talk about a redemption arc. It's
no secret that Andor has been a pretty
divisive show over the past couple of
years. Its slow pace, gritty, but
infrequent action, labyrinth themed
storylines, and complex, mature, and
yeah, slightly sterile characters didn't
fit all that well with a franchise that
was always about grand adventures, epic
storytelling, big emotional beats, and
clearly defined good and evil. Season 1
was wellreceived critically, but failed
to make much of an impact on the Star
Wars fan base. And I've got to admit,
season 2 didn't get off to a great start
either, opening with a trilogy of
episodes that felt weirdly clunky and
poorly written, badly paced, wasting too
much time on stuff that wasn't
interesting or compelling and capped off
by a controversial assault scene that
ended up overshadowing everything else
about the show, and probably driving a
big chunk of the fan base away at the
same time. It seemed like we might well
be heading for another Last of Us
situation. Another show that opens
strong only to squander it with a crappy
second season that failed to cement its
success. But then something interesting
happened andor got good.
What? What the [ __ ] Episodes four,
five, and six really began to move the
plot along, bringing together all the
wandering storylines that felt so
disjointed at the start of the season,
gradually ramping up the pace and the
stakes and the tension. The show was
building up to something big and we knew
it. Suddenly, I was getting invested
again. I was eager to see where the
story was going next and I began to feel
like we were in good hands once again.
Then we got last week's episodes.
Proceed. You know, there's probably
hundreds of fancy, pretentious ways that
I could eulogize this show, but what the
hell, let's just call a spade a spade
here. Andor is really [ __ ] good. And
I don't mean slightly better than the
usual bottom of the barrel Disney Star
Wars slop kind of good. I mean, sitting
on the edge of your seat in complete
silence knowing you're watching the sort
of TV event that only comes along once
every few years kind of goods. I mean,
this is some of the best Star Wars I've
seen since the original trilogy kind of
goods. And even now, I can't quite
believe I'm actually saying this about
anything made under Disney Star Wars,
but what the [ __ ] Credit where credit's
due, and this show deserves a [ __ ] ton
of it. Everything that's been building
up since the start of the season all
comes to fruition in these three
episodes. every plot line that seemed so
meandering, every character you'd begun
to question their purpose, every
decision and little piece of setup, it
all gets paid off right here. And I
guess what I'm saying is it's been worth
it. It's all been worth it. Honestly, I
could spend hours going over every
single character beat and plot point,
but for the sake of not getting too
self-indulgent, I'm going to try and
stick to the main events here. The
premise of this episode arc is that the
Empire needs a rare mineral to finish
construction of the Death Star, a
mineral that can only be found in large
quantities on one planet, Gorman. A
wealthy and influential trading hub, and
not the kind of place they can just
march into and take over at will. See,
as powerful as they are, the Empire are
not omnipotent. They can't just do
whatever the [ __ ] they want and get away
with it. Instead, they have to play it
smart. Gradually isolating and turning
public opinion against Gorman, pushing
them closer and closer to the brink,
stirring up a rebellion that'll give
them the perfect excuse to pacify the
whole planet. This season's been all
about turning the planet into a giant
powder keg. All it takes is one spark to
ignite it. And that spark finally comes
in episode 8, where all the major
storylines and characters converge on
the central square in Gorman. Honestly,
if you want a textbook example of how to
marry complex manipulation with
slowbuilding tension, paos, character
drama, impending doom, and epic tragedy,
then I don't think you could do any
better than this episodes. I'm so
[ __ ] happy this episode got made. I'm
so glad they didn't compromise or dumb
it down or sanitize it. This is exactly
the way it needed to be. And god damn,
it makes for good viewing. watching the
Imperial forces methodically slot each
building block into place. The
propaganda manipulation, the quiet
deployment of military forces, the
barely disguised provocation of the
locals, the convenient opening they make
to let them into the central square, and
the false flag operation they have on
standby. You realize the cold,
calculating, ruthlessly efficient
machine that's at work here. This isn't
the cartoonish clown show from the
sequel trilogy or some pretentious
moron's idea of an intellectual super
villain. This is the real face of evil.
The monster in the shadows that swallows
you up by degrees. That takes just a
little bit more each time without anyone
realizing where it's all leading. That
sets the trap knowing you're eventually
going to walk into it. That's more
powerful, more intelligent, more cunning
than you. the one that wins because it
convinces everyone else that it's doing
the right thing. And nowhere is that
brought home more powerfully than this
scene where Sirill, a willing pawn of
the Empire throughout the whole show,
always convinced he's on the right side
of history, finally realizes that he's
being played, that it was all a big lie.
How long have you known about all this?
How long? We can discuss this later.
Holy [ __ ] man. Nowhere else in Disney
Star Wars would they even think of
allowing a scene like this to happen.
You tell me it's a rumor and I will
throw you out that window. This is a man
whose entire worldview is collapsing in
front of his very eyes. A man who's
finally seeing the Empire for what it
really is. A man who's too late to stop
what's about to happen. Needless to say,
the Empire gets exactly what they
wanted. They get their massacre and
they're able to spin it into the
propaganda victory that they need to
take over the whole planet. I can't
emphasize enough how much work this show
has done to make the Empire a credible
threat again. They're not cartoonishly
evil for the sake of being evil, but
they are ruthless, intelligent, highly
organized, and brutally efficient at
pursuing their aims. And you can
absolutely see how and why they were
able to take control of an entire
galaxy. But as it turns out, they're not
the only ones who use the Gorman
massacre to their advantage. The
Rebellion also gamble everything to get
the word out on what's really happening
there, sacrificing their last shred of
political influence to light the spark
of revolution throughout the entire
galaxy. It is a fascinating culmination
of multiple storylines that have been
building up all season and in some cases
since the beginning of the show itself.
But just as important, it's also an
absolute emotional roller coaster that's
going to leave you feeling exhausted by
the ends. I'm not kidding when I say
that these episodes made me feel things
about a Star Wars project that I haven't
felt in 20 years, which is saying a lot
for a Scottish guy in his 40s who
doesn't experience normal human emotion
anymore. And it's pretty interesting now
to look back on all the criticism of
Andor being too clinical, too
intellectualized, too sterile for people
to get invested in. [ __ ] man. Some of
it even came from me. And although I
100% stand by what I said at the time,
that assessment inevitably changes once
you see a fuller picture of what the
show's trying to do. And well, now I
have. Those big emotional payoffs that
people expect from Star Wars were always
coming. But unlike other shows and
movies that try to force this stuff out
of cheap melodrama andor makes you wait
for it. It makes you work for it and it
forces you to pay attention. And part of
me wonders now if that's why a lot of
Star Wars fans have struggled to engage
with it. There's no getting around this.
It is not as accessible. It's not as
fantastical or epic or adventurous as so
many other Star Wars projects. There's
no force, no Jedi, no lightsabers, no
quirky aliens or moments of levity to
lighten the tone. No epic confrontations
or grand heroic gestures. Just people
with all of our frailties and our
strengths, our drives, our desires, and
our infinite complexities. People with
ideals and beliefs they're willing to
fight and sacrifice and die for. And all
of this brings me back to the question
that I asked a couple of weeks ago. What
even is Star Wars now? What does it mean
to be a Star Wars story? Do you want the
cheap and easy dopamine hit of seeing a
bunch of stuff you recognize paraded
across the screen, like the greatest
hits of some tired old band that lost a
creative spark decades ago? Or do you
want something more challenging,
complex, and mature that takes the
setting and the world that you remember
and asks, "How would this actually work
if it was for real?" I'm asking this
because the answers aren't as easy or
obvious as you might think. Like it or
not, there's a kind of magical bubble, a
mystique around classic Star Wars that's
only solidifying the more the original
trilogy fades into the mists of time. Or
maybe I should say what this franchise
has come to symbolize in people's minds.
And the more time that passes, the more
shitty projects that explode from
Lucasfilm's shattered rectum, the more
difficult it becomes for people to
accept ideas outside of that bubble.
Instead, it becomes easier to segregate
real Star Wars into some safe, secure,
untouchable comfort zone that nobody can
ever mess with and anything made since
about 2005 into some shitty, barely
acknowledged piece of fanfiction.
Something that might carry the Star Wars
name, but is no more connected to it
than an unlicensed bootleg sequel. And
therein lies the problem. Because if we
set such a narrow path for what Star
Wars is allowed to be now, then we lose
any chance for it to grow or mature or
experiment as a brand, we end up
ignoring extremely well-made stories
that have more effort, time, and passion
put into them than a dozen seasons of
Keyjang Dave Fona slop. And we send the
message to Disney that all we really
want is to keep rehashing the same
tired, predictable, played out themes
and ideas over and over again until
there's simply nothing left to exploit.
And I think maybe that's the real
tragedy of a show like Andor. Yeah, it's
getting rave reviews and its core fan
base clearly love it, but it's not going
to be enough to move the needle. We'll
probably never get another Star Wars
project of this scope, scale, and level
of ambition and storytelling maturity
ever again. This was it. This was the
one chance for Star Wars to grow into
something more, to expand beyond
lightsabers and space battles and goofy
looking aliens. And I suspect that
chance has just slipped away because
really, why would any company want to
invest hundreds of millions of dollars
into a show that nobody [ __ ] watches?
So, get ready for a lot more loweffort,
low-risk corporate slop because I think
that's the real future for Star Wars at
this point. Andor tried and it tried
valiantly, but I don't think it's going
to be
enough. Anyway, that's all I've got for
today.
Go away
